title: What is a Minimax Game?
concept: A minimax game is a contest where one player tries to minimize a shared score while the other tries to maximize it, and repeated play settles toward a balance point.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In a GAN, what is the discriminator's job?

options.text: To judge whether a piece of data is real or generated (fake)
To generate brand new fake data from scratch
To store the training dataset on disk

exercises.explain: Right! The discriminator is the network that judges real vs. fake — and that judging is about to become part of a bigger tug-of-war.

cards.title: The carnival number booth
cards.body: At a carnival booth, Mia picks a secret number from 1 to 100 and gives sneaky clues designed to keep Leo guessing far off. Leo studies the clues and tries to land as close to Mia's number as possible. Who's really in control here?

cards.title: What is a minimax game?
cards.body: A minimax game is a contest with one shared score, where one player tries to minimize it and the other tries to maximize it. Neither player fully controls the outcome — it depends on both of their moves together.

cards.title: The thermostat standoff
cards.body: Picture two roommates sharing one thermostat: one keeps nudging it down (minimize the temperature), the other keeps nudging it up (maximize it). Neither gets their perfect number — instead, the room settles at a temperature somewhere in between.

cards.title: Back to the number booth
cards.body: Round after round, Mia's clues get trickier (minimizing Leo's accuracy) while Leo's guesses get sharper (maximizing his own accuracy). Over many rounds, Leo's guesses stop getting much better or worse — the gap between his guess and Mia's number settles into a steady range.

cards.title: It's not about a final winner
cards.body: You might think a minimax game ends when one side finally 'wins' for good. In reality, both players keep adjusting every round, and most minimax games settle into a steady back-and-forth rather than a permanent victory.

left.text:
Wants the score LOW
e.g. Mia hiding the number
Adjusts to shrink success

right.text:
Wants the score HIGH
e.g. Leo guessing closely
Adjusts to grow success

exercises.prompt: In a minimax game, what does the 'maximizing' player try to do?

options.text: Push the shared score as high as possible
Push the shared score as low as possible
Ignore the score and play randomly

exercises.explain: The maximizer's whole strategy is built around increasing the shared score in their favor.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In a minimax game, both players want the shared score to move in the same direction.
exercises.explain: False. One player pushes the score up, the other pushes it down — that opposite pull is what makes it a minimax game.

exercises.sentence: In a minimax game, one player tries to ___ the shared score while the other tries to maximize it.
exercises.explain: The minimizing player is always trying to shrink the shared score, while the maximizer works to grow it.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario best describes a minimax game?

options.text: A locksmith who keeps improving locks while a hobbyist keeps trying (and failing) to pick them, both endlessly adjusting
Two friends taking turns rolling dice with no strategy involved
A student memorizing flashcards alone in a quiet room

exercises.explain: The locksmith and the hobbyist share one score (how often the lock holds) — one adjusts to raise it, the other to lower it. That push-and-pull is minimax.

exercises.prompt: A vending machine keeps raising its price when it sees fewer thefts, and a group of students keeps finding new cheap tricks to grab snacks anyway. Which best explains this as a minimax game?

options.text: The machine tries to maximize the 'cost' students pay, while the students try to minimize it — both keep adjusting toward a stand-off
The machine and the students are working toward the exact same goal
There's no shared score here, so it isn't a minimax game
The machine always wins permanently once it raises its price

exercises.explain: Exactly — one side pushes a shared value up, the other pushes it down, and both keep adjusting. Next up: GANs play this very same push-and-pull, just with a generator and a discriminator instead of a machine and students!
